speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Henry Wolfgang Meyer. Today's date is November 20, 1995. The interviewer is Susan Harkavy. The city is Cincinnati, Ohio. The country is the United States, and the language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You crawled to the other side of the street.

speaker: interviewee
text: There couldn't be more of a difference as from this side to the other. We didn't quite realize it yet, but for instance, there was this big cannon operated by a black man, which was semi-automatic. He was doing something. And after a certain amount of time, the thing fired.

speaker: interviewee
text: Every time it fired, one member of our group, this Hungarian who was a little bit older than we were-- quite a bit older-- would fall over. Then he would take all his strengths to get up again, and that was next time for the cannon to fire. So I took all my English together, school English, and I went to the guy and said, couldn't you wait a little bit? We are from concentration camp. And the guy said, oh, my god, excuse me. And he got out of his pockets-- and you know, soldiers, [INAUDIBLE] all in his pockets-- everything he could give-- have, and give to us-- cigarettes, and chocolate, and rations, and what have you. And he said, oh, my god. What should we do? I said, I would like to see the commanding officers. So he got them-- got him. And I was questioned. And I could answer a few things in English. And then another soldier was standing by. He said, do you know a fellow by the name of [INAUDIBLE]? He said, no.

speaker: interviewee
text: He looked very sad. And then he went away, came back, and said, do you know a fellow by the name of Kurt [? Motulsky. ?] I said, of course I do. His parents had the store next to ours. They lived in our neighborhood. One of his brothers was my youth leader in the Jewish school. And I had a crush on his sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I mean, how much more can you know about it? And I told a few things. And the guy went to the other soldier and said, yes. And then he went away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. I told it wrong. He was brought and saw me, yeah. And I told the whole story. And then he went to another soldier said yes, yes, and went away.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a peculiar way of greeting somebody who has come out of concentration camps. And I don't-- what-- where have they been? Well, three years later, when I arrived in the United States, I spent my first evening with his parents and entire family. And he looked at me and said, my god. I didn't recognize you at all. Now I can see who you are. But everything you said was so right. So I said, yes, must be you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, if he wouldn't have done that, I don't know what would have happened. But this way, I was immediately-- my identity was established. And I was brought a little bit later. General Patton wanted me to become the chief of police at Bad Hersfeld in Germany. And he was very mad that I turned the offer down. And we were brought to Luxembourg, and to New York, and to Paris. And I was received by General Eisenhower for special interrogation. I also had seen General Eisenhower in Ohrdruf before, when he visited with General-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] another block of a name-- very famous, very sympatico man. He died about two years ago. They both visited the camp when I was together with all the prisoners to explain things. Well, there I was. On the 10th of April, I was liberated. I escaped on the 3rd. On the 10th, I was liberated. And about two weeks later, I was in Paris. My head was going this way. It was incredible. I was really alive. I was in Paris.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was received by Eisenhower. And he ask what I want to do. He said-- I said, I would like to go to the United States. And I have to find my relatives. Can we help? I gave him the information I had, I could remember. And the officers of Eisenhower found the cousins of my father. And pretty soon, I got an affidavit.

speaker: interviewee
text: By then, I was sort of coming back to life in Paris, through help of new friends, through help of people who had also survived, and so on. So one day, a friend from camp, who was spoke French, where I didn't speak French. I spoke English and not German, if I could. And a friend came and said, do you know, we have to go to get the [FRENCH].

speaker: interviewee
text: [FRENCH] means having coming back to your homeland. I didn't know that. So we went. And I spoke only English. And they said, well, how can he be [FRENCH] if he doesn't speak French, or something? And before I knew, I was in prison, in Paris, and-- on a Friday.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing happened on the Saturday. Nothing happened on the Sunday. Monday doesn't happen anything, for sure. Tuesday, finally, I got out. I lived, then, in an English home. The Americans had put me there. But they knew about me. And on Tuesday, I got out again. And grotesque is that, when you go out from my apartment, you will see a painting of Paris on the wall. It's the [INAUDIBLE]. It's the place where I was imprisoned. But that just came by coincidence. But you can imagine getting out of all of that and being imprisoned again in Paris, not knowing anybody, not the language, [INAUDIBLE]. Anyway, it was a mistake. They thought that they had gotten two SS members which tried to be hidden in Paris. And I was the third one. I showed them my number on the arm, and nothing helped till an American lawyer got me out. [DRINKS] Well, there I was in Paris. I put my fiddle back under the chin. I tried how well I could scratch at this moment. I met friends who bought me a violin on a [FRENCH], which is the flea market. And I started being a normal human being, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and waited for my American immigration, my visa. By this time, nobody could really go to America except Jewish people, because the German quota was blocked. But I had no papers, nothing. I had to find them from people and connection. And so, you know how much time that takes. But then everything was ready. And whenever I came back there, it was said, well, everything is fine. But now you need this one, because it's outdated. So I made the acquaintance of a very fine American musician, Julius Katchen, who, unfortunately, died much too early. And he said, look, what we do is we go and play a concert at the embassy. And when the ambassador comes, you tell him you applied. And this is how it happened. The ambassador said, [INAUDIBLE] come tomorrow morning at 11:00.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I did. And he had checked with the consul. And there was my visa. Turned out, this consul was opposed to immigration and was holding a lot of people [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I arrived in America, another completely new station. But by then, I had met, already, a number of American musicians. And I auditioned for the Juilliard school. And I got a scholarship at Juilliard. And I met my later on colleagues, especially Walter Levin, who was from Berlin. And we got along immediately very well. We both had the same ambition, to play a string quartet, to make chamber music, and so on. And I sort of auditioned for this quartet which he had. And there it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a member of the LaSalle Quartet. Now, I'm sure you're going to ask me, why LaSalle? Well, when we-- we are students for one year at Juilliard, the first violinist of the Juilliard Quartet, Robert Mann, was sitting in a telephone booth in a drugstore and negotiating a concert for his quartet. And the lady gave him a date. And he said, I'm sorry. We can't take that date, because we have already a concert. But I could recommend a young quartet. They are students of ours at Juilliard. And the woman said, that's fine with me. Who are they?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have a name. So he looked out from the drugstore, and it says, Broadway, LaSalle Street. And that's how we got stuck with that name. It's a very good name. And so, we were one year at Juilliard. We left Juilliard, became quartet in residence at Colorado College in Colorado. Stayed four years there. I thought that was wonderful for me. Because if you are in New York and you were together with former German refuges and all kinds of other-- you don't really become part of this country. But if you are living in Colorado, that's the right way. And from there, we came to Cincinnati. And I was 40 years professor at the university. I parted as distinguished professor, with the Dolly Cohen award. And I'm an adjunct professor now. And Cincinnati is a fantastic city, as far as I am concerned. It has been very good to us, has lots to offer. And not too much to offer, like New York, when you finally stop and said, I'm not going to do anything. I'll stay home. And it is now [? hub ?] station for Delta-- very easily reached. And you can go all place in no time whatsoever. And the population of Cincinnati, the people interested in our quartet, have been very, very good to us. And I'm very happy to be here. And this is my story. I think that the project of Mr. Spielberg is sensational. It's-- these times have not disappeared yet. And there are still people alive who went through that, like me. And already, people are told that this is all propaganda. Nothing of the sort was true. I mention that because, for more than 20, 25 years, I did not talk about these things. I thought, people don't understand me anyway. The, the human imagination doesn't go so far to imagine what has happened all to us. I just report what I still have in my memory.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I don't know if other people have an imagination to think in what kind of situation we were. Ergo, we have to put it on a record. Now, I didn't want to talk about it, because I didn't want to have my career sort of spotted with always pity, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would try something. They will say, it's amazing what he's doing after what he went through. So I didn't mention it. But then came the day when I read in a newspaper that a teacher was condemned because she said, this never happened, Holocaust, it's just a [INAUDIBLE]. And I was sitting with a friend of mine, a very famous pianist. And I was telling him, look-- I was very upset. Look what she's saying or something. And he said to me, Henry, it's your fault, partly. And me, who had hoped to get some pity from him for this sad situation, he says it's my fault.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I wasn't very happy. But then I thought about it. He said, if you do not report, if you don't tell it to the world, we never will know. And that has made me speak about it. And I hope that there are a number of things in my interview which will be on record. Wonderful. Can we hear a little of your LaSalle Quartet, while we show your pictures, for the rest of the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I gave you-- I give you record. I think it's better.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: Put it on the tape. That's fine. Great.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Should we do it now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that OK with you, Jerry? While we get the pictures?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Um. [MUSIC PLAYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: And go ahead, sir. Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is a photo of my brother and me, I would say about 1937. And-- or-- that isn't quite right, 1936 at the most, because I have not been bar mitzvahed yet. And this is in our summer dress. My parents liked to design special clothes for us. And it was always made by a [NON-ENGLISH] for my mother, who made [INAUDIBLE]. Now, this is somewhere in the summer. I don't know anymore where, but I'm sure it's in Dresden. And there is one more, which is just the opposite season.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Should I move it?

speaker: interviewer
text: No. That's fine. Did you say if you're on the left or right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Which-- which one are you? Are you on the right or the--

speaker: interviewee
text: I am the one on the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The older one. And this one is about the same time, but in the winter, either the winter before or the winter after. I would say the winter before. And again, you can see that we have sort of special coats made. And then, when it was done, I sort of hated it. But now, I sort of get a kick out of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you could come up a little bit with that picture so that you can see the naked knees, which belonged to the German trend. You see? Now, if you go to the next photo, which-- here again, at this photo, I was at the right and my brother the left. These are my parents, about the same time. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] if you go to this one--

speaker: interviewer
text: These are your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: After my-- well, the name of my parents is Harry [? Haber ?] Meyer and Johanna, born Schifter. And it's in the street where we lived. I remember that. And if you go to this photo, that is about two years later. And that was already the time when I was in Prague studying. And I was home for a visit.

speaker: interviewer
text: You are which one?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am the one on the left this time.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were 15, right-- around 15, 16?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I'm around 15. And this photo, yeah, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this is the same time as the other one. Because this is taken in the room in which I lived in Prague with the family [? factor. ?] If you go back to this photo, where I'm alone on it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just want to get them-- make sure I have enough of this one.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and now--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was about at the age of 13. I think it was when I had bar mitzvah. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I found all these pictures during the last year. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This one was the first photo after I had left the concentration camp. And it was my permit to be-- to live in Paris.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the latest photo which came to me is from a German-Jewish newspaper in the United States. And it showed the opening of the Jewish school in Dresden. And to my greatest surprise, somebody sent that article to me. And I found my father and myself on this picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it. That's my father. And on the left, it's me. And it's the only picture I have of my father and me.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's the only picture you have of your father and you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's amazing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that, also, only by absolute coincidence.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you very much, Mr. Meyer.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Look at this. Watch this. It's like if you, if you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Blur it?

speaker: interviewer
text: If you blur it, you can see it better.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: See that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: There's sharp focus, and you can't--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can see that, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's good.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you blur it. Isn't that weird?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old do you think you were in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: '39-- [INAUDIBLE] 1939, 16 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait. [? Wait a minute. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just when I had gotten back from Prague.
